title: The Boy Who Knew Too Much

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Homer crashes car in opening credits
jokes.punchline: His signature frustrated exclamation
jokes.explanation: Iconic Homer catchphrase that became cultural phenomenon. Perfect character integration and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Oh! How can they imprison kids in school on a beautiful day like this?
jokes.setup: Beautiful day outside, kids going to school
jokes.punchline: Bart's dramatic characterization of school as prison
jokes.explanation: Standard Bart anti-authority sentiment. Functional character comedy but not original.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Man, look at that beautiful day. All those kids going to school... locked up like prisoners.

jokes.text: Prison bus, Otto? - The regular school bus broke down. So take a seat before I blow your heads off! - Otto! - Oh, sorry. This bus and I have sort of a Shining thing going on.
jokes.setup: School bus is actually a prison bus
jokes.punchline: Otto casually references The Shining's psychological horror as explanation for violent outburst
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy mixing Otto's laid-back personality with horror movie reference. The casual delivery of violent threat followed by pop culture explanation is perfectly structured.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Otto, why'd you just try to run over Principal Skinner?

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: All work and no play, man. You know, like The Shining.

jokes.text: Hey, Huck, what's L-I-N-C-O-N doing here? I don't know. It's your fantasy. - Hi, Abe. - Hello, Bart.
jokes.punchline: Characters acknowledge it's a fantasy and break fourth wall
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-humor with characters acknowledging their fictional status within the fantasy.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wow, I'm actually in a fantasy with Huck Finn and Abraham Lincoln!

dialogue.speaker: Huck Finn
dialogue.line: This here's mighty peculiar, boy.

dialogue.speaker: Abraham Lincoln
dialogue.line: Indeed, this defies all logic and reason.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: You guys realize this is just a fantasy sequence, right? We're in a cartoon.

dialogue.speaker: Huck Finn
dialogue.line: Well, I'll be. We're fictional characters in a fictional scene.

dialogue.speaker: Abraham Lincoln
dialogue.line: How delightfully meta. The fourth wall crumbles before us.

jokes.text: Well, children, our new ultra-hard Posturific chairs have arrived. They've been designed by eminent posturologists... to eliminate slouching by the year 3000.
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously named 'Posturific' chairs with thousand-year timeline for effectiveness
jokes.explanation: Good satirical jab at educational bureaucracy with invented word 'Posturific' and absurd timeline.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: We've invested in new chairs for the school. They're called 'Posturific' chairs.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: According to the manufacturer, they'll improve student posture within a thousand years.

jokes.text: I know they seem a little uncomfortable right now. But eventually your bones will change shape. I've lost all feeling in the left side of my body.
jokes.setup: Teacher explaining uncomfortable chairs will eventually work
jokes.punchline: Student immediately reports severe physical harm
jokes.explanation: Effective dark comedy escalation from mild discomfort to serious medical emergency.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Krabappel
dialogue.line: These chairs may be uncomfortable now, but your bodies will adapt to them.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: My doctor says I have the spine of a 90-year-old man!

jokes.text: Now, unfortunately our school clocks have been running fast all semester... so today we all have to stay two extra hours to make up for the time we lost.
jokes.punchline: Backwards logic that fast clocks mean students owe more time
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist logic that satirizes institutional incompetence and bureaucratic reasoning.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Krabappel
dialogue.line: Well, if the school clocks are running fast, that means you students are actually leaving early. So technically, you owe the school more time.

jokes.text: And to think I got all this after dropping out of the fourth grade.
jokes.punchline: Reveals his minimal education as source of success
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific humor playing on Otto's lack of education leading to modest job satisfaction.

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Whoa, check out this fancy car! With leather seats and a sunroof and everything!

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: You know what? I didn't even finish high school, man. And look at me now!

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: I'm living the dream, dude. No diploma, no problem!

jokes.text: That's okay, Bart. I trust you.
jokes.punchline: Teacher's naive trust despite transparent lie
jokes.explanation: Simple ironic moment but nothing exceptional. Sets up the contrast with Skinner's suspicion.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mrs. Krabappel, I can't do my homework because my dog ate it.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Krabappel
dialogue.line: Oh, that's perfectly alright, Bart. I understand completely.

jokes.text: Please excuse my handwriting. I busted whichever hand it is I write with. Signed, Mrs. Simpson.
jokes.punchline: Fake note with deliberately vague and suspicious details
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Bart's lazy approach to deception. The vagueness makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dear Principal Skinner, Please excuse Bart from school yesterday. He was sick with a case of... uh... the grumblies. He'll be back when he feels better. Sincerely, Homer Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: The grumblies? Homer, there's no such thing as the grumblies.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's what makes it so brilliant, man. He'd never suspect it.

jokes.text: Inconclusive. I wish more students had agreed to these electronic tracking implants. We only had one volunteer.
jokes.setup: Skinner analyzing handwriting in crime lab
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions dystopian student tracking implants
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy showing Skinner's authoritarian tendencies through casual mention of tracking implants.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: As you can see, the handwriting matches perfectly. We've identified the culprit.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Of course, with the new student tracking implants we installed last semester, we wouldn't even need handwriting analysis. We know where every student is at all times.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: It's quite efficient, really. And the screaming stops after a few days.

jokes.text: Spill it! Where's your brother? You'd better answer him, Lisa. He's a good cop/bad cop routine gone wrong
jokes.setup: Interrogation scene with good cop/bad cop
jokes.punchline: Lisa points out they've switched roles and gotten confused
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-humor where character awareness of the trope becomes the joke itself.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Now listen here, we're going to play good cop, bad cop.

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Aye, and I'll be the bad cop! STOP YOUR BLATHERIN'!

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Willie, you're supposed to be the good cop.

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Well, I dinna know! Ye said bad cop first!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Actually, you've both gotten so confused, you've completely switched roles.

jokes.text: My fantasy's come true!
jokes.setup: Bart seeing something that delights him
jokes.punchline: His excitement over what's probably something inappropriate for a kid
jokes.explanation: Standard Bart reaction. Works as setup but not particularly clever on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba! A Playdude magazine!

jokes.text: Hey, kid. You wanna see a dead body?
jokes.setup: Adult addressing kid at what seems like innocent location
jokes.punchline: Disturbing offer presented casually
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that subverts expectations of normal adult-child interaction.

jokes.text: If I were a truant boy out for a good time, I'd be right here-- the Springfield Natural History Museum.
jokes.punchline: His misunderstanding of what kids find fun
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Skinner's disconnect from student interests.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: I'm looking for a truant student. Have you seen a young boy around here?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No, sir. But if you're looking for fun, I know where all the kids hang out.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Excellent! Take me there at once.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It's the library.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: The library? But that's so... educational.

jokes.text: Look, if I was under 17, I'd be in school, right? Yeah, I guess you're right. Enjoy Boob-A-Rama, sir.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to get into adult venue using fake logic
jokes.punchline: Employee buys it and reveals the venue's crude name
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with Bart's reverse psychology and the reveal of the establishment's name.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Look, I'm 12 years old, which means I'm one year away from 13. And 13 is a teenager. So technically, I'm already a teenager.

dialogue.speaker: Theater employee
dialogue.line: You know what? That actually makes sense. Welcome to the Backdoor Sluts 9 theater.

jokes.text: Why, there are no children here at the 4-H Club either. Am I so out of touch? No. It's the children who are wrong.
jokes.setup: Skinner questioning his understanding of children
jokes.punchline: His immediate reversal to blame children instead
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy that became a cultural meme. Perfect structure showing Skinner's inability for self-reflection. The quick reversal from doubt to arrogant certainty is perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Good Lord, I've been thinking about this all wrong. Perhaps the children aren't the problem at all. Maybe it's the system that's failed them.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: No, wait. It's definitely the children.

jokes.text: Yes. Were there any serious bids for this painting?
jokes.punchline: Auctioneer dismisses millions as non-serious bids
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan humor about art world pretension and inflated values.

dialogue.speaker: Auctioneer
dialogue.line: Do I hear two million? Two million dollars? Going once... going twice...

dialogue.speaker: Auctioneer
dialogue.line: Sold! To the gentleman in the back for two million dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Auctioneer
dialogue.line: Now, we have some spirited bidding from Homer Simpson. Sir, I'm afraid we don't accept payment in donuts and IOUs.

jokes.text: Can't let Dad see me playing hooky! Can't let the boy see me skippin' work. Good afternoon. How do you do, sir? Sucker!
jokes.setup: Father and son both where they shouldn't be
jokes.punchline: Both put on fake politeness then each think they fooled the other
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy showing the family similarity between Bart and Homer. The mutual deception and satisfaction is perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh, hi Dad. What a surprise to see you here.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I was just... uh... checking on things. Perfectly innocent.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Of course. I'm sure you have a very good reason for being here.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I do. A very good reason. And you? What brings you to this location?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh, you know, just passing through. Nothing to worry about.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Excellent. Well, I'm glad we cleared that up. You're a good kid.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Thanks, Dad. You're a great father.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I sure fooled him.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That was easier than I thought.

jokes.text: A spoor! His brand of gum, Doublemint. Trying to double your fun, eh, Bart? Well, I'll double your detention.
jokes.setup: Skinner tracking Bart finds gum wrapper
jokes.punchline: Elaborate pun connecting gum slogan to detention threat
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay showing Skinner's pedantic nature, though the pun is somewhat forced.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: A gum wrapper? This is exactly the kind of evidence I need!

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Simpson, it seems you've been chewing gum in the hallways. I'm afraid I'm going to have to give you detention.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: And I suggest you remember: the choice is yours, but so are the consequences!

jokes.text: I wish someone was around to hear that.
jokes.punchline: His disappointment that no one witnessed his clever wordplay
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-humor where Skinner's awareness of his own wit becomes the joke. Perfect character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: I just made an elaborate pun and no one was around to hear it. Do you know how depressing that is? It's like a tree falling in the forest with no one there to appreciate my superior wit.

jokes.text: And so we enter endgame.
jokes.setup: Chase sequence with kid skipping school
jokes.punchline: Skinner's dramatic chess metaphor for truancy pursuit
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Skinner's tendency to over-dramatize mundane situations.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: A student skips school? It's like a chess match. I'm the grandmaster, and truancy is my opponent. I've already anticipated three moves ahead!

jokes.text: Oh, my God! He is like some sort of... non-giving up... school guy.
jokes.setup: Bart being pursued relentlessly by Skinner
jokes.punchline: His struggle to articulate 'relentless' or 'persistent'
jokes.explanation: Excellent character humor showing Bart's limited vocabulary leading to awkward but funny description.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Man, Skinner is like, relentless... or... persistent... or whatever.

jokes.text: Oh, he's close. I can taste his fear.
jokes.punchline: His predatory language about catching a kid
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Skinner's obsessive pursuit with inappropriately intense language.

jokes.text: Freddy, honey. I think something just dropped into the backseat. I'm not paying you to talk.
jokes.setup: Bart falls into car, woman mentions it
jokes.punchline: Freddy's rude dismissal reveals their relationship dynamic
jokes.explanation: Establishes Freddy's character as crude and dismissive, setting up later events.

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: Bart fell into your car.

dialogue.speaker: Freddy Quimby
dialogue.line: So?

jokes.text: People, the punch has been spiked. That's my nephew... displaying the Quimby wit that's won the public's heart.
jokes.setup: Freddy announces spiked punch at party
jokes.punchline: Uncle's proud reaction to what's basically underage drinking facilitation
jokes.explanation: Good political satire showing Mayor's willingness to spin anything positively.

dialogue.speaker: Freddy Quimby
dialogue.line: I spiked the punch!

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: That's my boy!

jokes.text: Happy birthday, Freddy. And may all your disgraces be, uh, private.
jokes.punchline: Cynical wish that scandals stay hidden rather than for good behavior
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire in the form of a toast. Captures politician's cynical worldview perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: To my nephew on his birthday, I wish that all his indiscretions remain hidden from the press and the authorities.

jokes.text: And who are you, little boy? I'm one of your nephews you don't see very often, uh, Bart-Bart.
jokes.punchline: His improvised fake identity with doubled name
jokes.explanation: Standard Bart improvisation under pressure. The name doubling is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: And who might you be, young man?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Bart Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Bart Bart? What an unusual name!

jokes.text: Hey, McBain, I'm a big fan, but your last movie really sucked. I know. There were script problems from day one. Yeah, I'll say. Magic ticket my ass, McBain.
jokes.setup: Kid talking to action star at party
jokes.punchline: Actor agreeing with criticism and kid doubling down
jokes.explanation: Good satire of Hollywood with actor's honest admission of poor quality.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: You know, McBain, your movies are pretty violent.

dialogue.speaker: McBain
dialogue.line: You're right, kid. They are too violent.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, and your acting is terrible too.

jokes.text: Maria, my mighty heart is breaking. I'll be in the Humvee.
jokes.punchline: Over-dramatic reaction with incongruous military vehicle reference
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy mixing melodrama with military vehicle absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: McBain
dialogue.line: They said my acting was wooden! Wooden! I'm going to kill them all with this tank!

jokes.text: Hey, what the hell is this? It is a bowl of 'shao-dair,' sir. Wait a minute. Come here. What did you call it? Say it loud enough so everyone can hear. Come on. Say it.
jokes.setup: Rich kid demanding explanation of food
jokes.punchline: Aggressive interrogation over pronunciation
jokes.explanation: Sets up the central conflict while establishing Freddy as a bully about cultural differences.

dialogue.speaker: Freddy Quimby
dialogue.line: What is this? Some kind of joke?

dialogue.speaker: French waiter
dialogue.line: Non, monsieur, it is 'Coq au Vin.'

dialogue.speaker: Freddy Quimby
dialogue.line: Cock? You're calling me a cock?

dialogue.speaker: French waiter
dialogue.line: Non, non, 'Coq au Vin' — it is chicken in wine sauce.

dialogue.speaker: Freddy Quimby
dialogue.line: I know what it is, I just don't know why you're being so hostile about the pronunciation!

jokes.text: 'Shao-dair'? 'Shao-dair'? It's 'chowdah.' Say it right! 'Shah-der.' Come back here! I'm not through demeaning you!
jokes.punchline: Freddy's explicit admission that he's 'demeaning' someone
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work - Freddy's unconscious admission of his cruel intent is perfectly delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Freddy Quimby
dialogue.line: I'm not demeaning you, I'm simply stating facts.

dialogue.speaker: French waiter
dialogue.line: You called me a 'frog-eater'!

dialogue.speaker: Freddy Quimby
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but I wasn't being demeaning about it.

jokes.text: Wow! This is the biggest Rice Krispy square I've ever seen. Boy, the rich sure know how to live.
jokes.setup: Bart at fancy party observing luxury
jokes.punchline: His amazement at oversized basic snack food
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's working-class perspective on wealth through simple pleasures.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa! Check it out, man. They've got those giant shrimp things.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: And look at the size of these pigs in a blanket! They're like, three feet long!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, this is insane. It's like they took regular food and just made it huge.

jokes.text: Oh, my God! Someone's taken a bite out of the big Rice Krispy square! Oh, yeah, and the waiter's been brutally beaten.
jokes.punchline: Prioritizing vandalized snack over human suffering
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy prioritizing property damage over human injury. Perfect callback structure.

dialogue.speaker: Party guest
dialogue.line: Oh my God, someone's been murdered! And they ate all the guacamole!

jokes.text: It says Freddy Quimby beat a waiter half to death. Those Quimby children are so wild and rich. I hope he finally gets what's coming to him.
jokes.punchline: Casual acceptance of rich family's criminal behavior
jokes.explanation: Standard class commentary through character reactions. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my, another assault by a wealthy family member. How shocking.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, rich people can do whatever they want. That's why they're rich.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But Homer, that's not how the law works!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure it is. The more money you have, the less the rules apply. It's simple economics.

jokes.text: But Freddy Quimby's innocent! How do you know? There weren't any witnesses. Oh, yeah. Right. You'd think someone would've seen something at a crowded party like that. Well, they didn't, okay? They didn't!
jokes.punchline: Bart's increasingly defensive denials despite obviously being the witness
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony with Bart's obvious guilt about his secret knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So nobody saw who ate all the donuts?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, Bart was in the kitchen...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I wasn't even there! I was... uh... outside!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But we found donut crumbs on your shirt.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Those could be anyone's crumbs! They're very common crumbs!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, there's a half-eaten donut in your back pocket.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: A plant! Someone planted it on me! This is a setup!

jokes.text: Oh, look, Charlie Brown said, 'Good grief.' I didn't see that comin'.
jokes.setup: Homer reading comics after tense conversation
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic surprise at extremely predictable comic strip
jokes.explanation: Nice meta-humor about comic strip predictability. Good tension break.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, a realistic comic strip about the struggles of everyday life. How refreshingly original!

jokes.text: Lisa, I gotta tell somebody. I was at the Quimby compound yesterday when that frog waiter got whacked. I know that Freddy Quimby is innocent.
jokes.punchline: Referring to French waiter as 'frog waiter'
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay mixing ethnic slur with literal description. More plot function than comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I know something you don't know.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That waiter is French.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So I'm gonna call him 'frog waiter' for the rest of the meal.

jokes.text: Bart Simpson, I know you cut school yesterday. And as soon as I can prove it, I'm shipping you off... to the Christian Military Reform School.
jokes.punchline: Extremely specific and severe school as threat
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Skinner's authoritarian fantasies with specific institutional name.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: You better shape up, or I'll make you stay after school and alphabetize the library by the dewey decimal system, then reorganize it by my personal preference, which changes hourly. And you'll do it in complete silence while I watch you with this ruler, occasionally tapping it against my palm. Then I'll assign you detention essays: 500 words on why gum isn't allowed, 500 words on why running isn't allowed, and 500 words on why talking about how unfair these assignments are isn't allowed. And if you so much as sigh, we start over from the beginning!

jokes.text: Well, if Freddy Quimby didn't do it... I'm sure he'll be found innocent by a fair and impartial jury. Oh, jury duty! I'll see that Quimby kid hang for this!
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately proves her wrong with biased reaction
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic timing with Lisa's idealism immediately undercut by Homer's prejudice.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The justice system is designed to protect the innocent and ensure fairness for all.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, that's nice, sweetie. Now if you'll excuse me, I need to go yell at the TV about how the refs are biased against our team.

jokes.text: I knew it was a bad idea to watch him open the mail.
jokes.setup: Homer's violent reaction to jury duty
jokes.punchline: Marge's understated regret about the decision
jokes.explanation: Good Marge moment with understated reaction to obvious problem.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I really think you should take jury duty seriously.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Jury duty? That's for people with time on their hands!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It's your civic responsibility.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Civic schmic! I'm not going!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose I shouldn't have encouraged you to go.

jokes.text: Behind these doors, a federal judge will ladle out steaming bowls... of rich, creamy justice in a case the media have dubbed... 'Beat-up Waiter.' This reporter suggested 'Waitergate,' but was shouted down at the press club.
jokes.punchline: Extended food metaphor and reporter's wounded pride over rejected pun
jokes.explanation: Good media satire with elaborate food metaphor and meta-commentary on headline creation.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: This trial has been like a soufflé - delicate, complex, and liable to collapse at any moment. The prosecution's case? Overcooked. The defense's arguments? Half-baked. And the judge's rulings? Well, let's just say they're not exactly cooking with gas.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: I tried to tell them earlier - I said this case was 'a recipe for disaster' - but did anyone appreciate the culinary wordplay? No. No one did.

jokes.text: I know you can read my thoughts, Bart. Just a little reminder-- if I find out you cut class... your ass is mine. Yes, you heard me. I think words I would never say.
jokes.punchline: His awareness of his own internal profanity
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor with Skinner's self-awareness of his internal moral contradiction.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Why you little... I'm going to... (internal monologue) Oh, the words I would say if I weren't such a well-mannered individual!

jokes.text: Meow meow meow meow Meow meow meow meow
jokes.setup: Bart trying to block out Skinner's mental threats
jokes.punchline: Humming/meowing to himself as mental defense
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag showing Bart's childish coping mechanism.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: La la la la la...

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Bart, I'm thinking very hard about you right now.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Meow meow meow meow meow meow meow!

jokes.text: That waiter, Mr. LaCoste, not only wasn't born in Springfield... he wasn't even born in this country!
jokes.punchline: Xenophobic appeal presented as legitimate legal argument
jokes.explanation: Good satire of prejudicial legal tactics disguised as relevant evidence.

dialogue.speaker: Defense Attorney
dialogue.line: Your Honor, my client is clearly innocent. After all, he's not one of those... foreign types.

jokes.text: Uh, Freddy Quimby was with me the entire... night in question. We were collecting canned goods for the starving people in... uh, you know, one of them loser countries.
jokes.punchline: His charitable alibi undercut by casual xenophobia
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Mayor's attempt at virtue signaling undermined by his prejudice.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: I was at the orphanage that night, reading to the children. Very charitable work, I might add.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Of course, we had to ask the foreign ones to leave first. They were making the other children uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Oh, good. My laundry is done. You see, Lisa? They don't need my testimony. Only because Mayor Quimby is buying his nephew's freedom. The system works. Just ask Claus von Bulow.
jokes.punchline: Bart's naive faith in system with reference to famous acquitted defendant
jokes.explanation: Good satire of justice system with historical reference to controversial acquittal.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The evidence was completely fabricated! The jury will see through this!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Are you kidding? The legal system always works, man. Just like O.J.!

jokes.text: Ladies and gentlemen, I'm going to prove to you not only that Freddy Quimby is guilty... but that he is also innocent of not being guilty.
jokes.punchline: Logically circular and meaningless argument
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist legal double-talk that sounds impressive but means nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Prosecutor
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the evidence is clear. My opponent claims innocence, but if he were innocent, he wouldn't need to claim innocence. And if he's claiming innocence, he must be guilty of something worth defending against. Therefore, he is guilty.

jokes.text: Well, only one in two million people has... what we call the 'evil gene.' Hitler had it. Walt Disney had it. And Freddy Quimby has it.
jokes.punchline: Pseudo-scientific 'evil gene' with Hitler-Disney comparison
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy linking Hitler and Disney as evil equivalents. Subverts Disney's wholesome image perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, I've isolated the evil gene. It's a combination of Hitler and Disney.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Disney?!

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: A ruthless businessman who smiles.

jokes.text: You rest your case? What? Oh, no. I thought that was just a figure of speech. Case closed.
jokes.punchline: His confusion about basic legal procedure
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment showing legal system incompetence.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Prosecutor, you've rested your case?

dialogue.speaker: Prosecutor
dialogue.line: Yes, Your Honor.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Then the defense may now present their case.

dialogue.speaker: Prosecutor
dialogue.line: Wait, they get a turn too?

jokes.text: He's wealthy, therefore, inherently good.
jokes.setup: Defense attorney's argument about client's character
jokes.punchline: Explicit statement of class bias
jokes.explanation: Sharp class satire making implicit bias explicit.

dialogue.speaker: Defense Attorney
dialogue.line: Your Honor, my client is a man of impeccable character. He's never been in trouble before.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: That may be true, but the evidence speaks for itself.

dialogue.speaker: Defense Attorney
dialogue.line: But sir, he comes from a good family. They're not wealthy, perhaps, but they're honest, hard-working people.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Well, that's the problem right there.

jokes.text: Well, therefore, you certainly would never lose your temper... over something as trivial as the pronunciation of 'chowder.' That's 'chowdah!' 'Chowdah!' I'll kill you! I'll kill all of you! Especially those of you in the jury!
jokes.punchline: Freddy immediately proves the point by exploding with rage
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup-payoff with Freddy destroying his own defense. Excellent callback to original incident.

dialogue.speaker: Defense Attorney
dialogue.line: Now, Mr. Quimby, your honor has suggested you have a temper problem. Is that true?

dialogue.speaker: Freddy Quimby
dialogue.line: Why you little—! I'll kill you! I'll kill all of you!

jokes.text: Wow, that didn't go well. The defense rests.
jokes.punchline: Attorney's massive understatement
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan reaction to complete legal disaster.

dialogue.speaker: Defense Attorney
dialogue.line: Well, that probably didn't help our case.

jokes.text: This won't take long. He's clearly guilty. We'll probably be home by dinnertime. Good. Tonight, the dog from Frasier is gonna ride the dolphin on Sea Quest.
jokes.punchline: Homer's excitement about absurd TV crossover
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's priorities and 90s TV culture reference.

dialogue.speaker: Juror
dialogue.line: I predict we'll have a verdict by lunch.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A verdict? But then I'll miss the crossover between Cops and Touched by an Angel!

jokes.text: Well, Bart, because of you... a horrible yet innocent person is going to jail.
jokes.punchline: Her accidentally accurate description of Freddy as 'horrible yet innocent'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment with Lisa's moral clarity leading to unintentionally perfect description.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You need to understand that your actions have consequences, Bart. Every choice you make ripples outward, affecting everyone around you.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, whatever.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Take Freddy, for example. He's horrible yet innocent—a walking contradiction of terrible behavior paired with complete obliviousness to the harm he causes.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, Lisa? That's like... exactly Freddy.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wait, was I being accurate?

jokes.text: Bart Simpson, for the heinous crime of hooky... I sentence you to a lifetime of hard labor in the cafeteria. More creamed corn, Jimbo Jr.? This creamed corn tastes like creamed crap. Watch the potty mouth, honey.
jokes.punchline: Bart aged into elderly cafeteria worker still being corrected for language
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation fantasy showing Skinner's vindictive imagination with good aging detail work.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: In my fantasy, Bart ages into an elderly cafeteria worker, still serving food at Springfield Elementary.

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Bart
dialogue.line: More gravy, eh?

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: It's 'gravy,' not 'eh,' Bart. Even at seventy years old, your grammar is atrocious.

jokes.text: I just wanted to say how great it is... to finally see some chicks on the bench. Keep up the good work, toots.
jokes.punchline: His inappropriate attempt at gender-based flattery to judge
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Bart's desperation and inappropriate social skills.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Did you or did you not pull the fire alarm?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Your Honor, with all due respect, that's a lovely pantsuit. Is that Chanel?

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: That's irrelevant, Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: And those earrings really bring out your eyes. Have you considered a career in modeling?

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Bailiff, remove him from my courtroom.

jokes.text: Chicks on the bench.
jokes.punchline: Judge's flat repetition showing her reaction
jokes.explanation: Simple reaction beat emphasizing the inappropriate nature of Bart's comment.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Bart Simpson, you are hereby sentenced to community service.

jokes.text: What does 'sequestered' mean? If the jury is deadlocked, they are put in a hotel together... so they can't communicate with the outside world. What does 'deadlocked' mean? It's when the jury can't agree on a verdict. Uh-huh. And 'if'? A conjunction meaning 'in the event that' or 'on condition that.'
jokes.punchline: His questions becoming increasingly basic, ending with asking definition of 'if'
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating stupidity joke that culminates perfectly with asking about 'if'.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What's a 'preponderance of the evidence'?

dialogue.speaker: Foreman
dialogue.line: It means the evidence supporting one side is heavier than the other.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What's 'evidence'?

dialogue.speaker: Foreman
dialogue.line: Things that prove something happened.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What's 'proof'?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What does 'if' mean?

jokes.text: So if we don't all vote the same way... we'll be deadlocked... and have to be sequestered... in the Springfield Palace Hotel. That's not gonna happen, Homer. Let's vote. My liver is failing.
jokes.punchline: His transparent scheme revealed, followed by juror's urgent health issue
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Homer's scheme with darkly comic urgency from dying juror.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. If the lemon tree is *his* lemon tree, then who's been eating my lemons?

dialogue.speaker: Other juror
dialogue.line: We've been deliberating for three hours.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I move we steal a car.

dialogue.speaker: Other juror
dialogue.line: What? No!

dialogue.speaker: Other juror
dialogue.line: Also, I'm having a stroke.

jokes.text: Where we'll get: a free room, free food, free swimming pool... free HBO-- Ooh! Free Willy! Justice is not a frivolous thing, Simpson. It has little, if anything, to do with a disobedient whale.
jokes.punchline: His excitement about movie and juror's stern correction about whale behavior
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay with 'Free Willy' and moral correction about disobedient whales.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh boy, a hotel with a movie channel! And they have room service, and a spa, and—

dialogue.speaker: Other juror
dialogue.line: Homer, we're supposed to be deliberating the case, not enjoying the amenities.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But did you see the whale documentary? It said whales can hold their breath for—

dialogue.speaker: Other juror
dialogue.line: That's not relevant to whether the defendant is guilty or innocent!

jokes.text: How many S's in 'innocent'? I'm only doing what I think is right. I believe Freddy Quimby should walk out of here a free hotel.
jokes.punchline: His slip revealing true motivation about 'free hotel'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Freudian slip revealing Homer's true motivation through malapropism.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose the evidence does seem to point to... wait, did you say free hotel?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Guilty! Guilty, guilty, guilty!

jokes.text: You know, we're kind of like the original Odd Couple. You're the messy one, and I'm-- Shut up! Oh, yes. Very well.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to make conversation with hotel roommate
jokes.punchline: His attempt at bonding immediately rejected
jokes.explanation: Standard rejection humor showing Homer's social awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So, uh, you come here often?

dialogue.speaker: Roommate
dialogue.line: We're in a hotel room together.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Right, right. So... what's your favorite type of donut?

dialogue.speaker: Roommate
dialogue.line: Please stop talking to me.

jokes.text: So, Bart... have your insides been gnawed away by guilt yet? Sell it to Hallmark, Sis. You're looking at cucumber boy. As in, 'cool as a.'
jokes.punchline: Bart's overwrought explanation of 'cool as a cucumber'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's defensive posturing and awkward explanation.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, why do you look so guilty?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Guilty? Me? I'm cool as a cucumber, man.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You're sweating profusely.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's just... cucumber juice, dude. Cucumbers are like, 95% water, so when you're as cool as one, you naturally perspire the essence of the cucumber.

jokes.text: You gotta tell him what you saw, Billy. But I'm so scared, McGarnigal. You gotta do this one for me, Billy. McGarnigal. Okay. For you, McGarnigal. Well, McGarnigal, Billy is dead! They slit his throat from ear to ear.
jokes.punchline: The witness is immediately murdered for testifying
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy meta-commentary on witness protection with immediate gruesome consequence.

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: And now, back to "Courthouse Chaos."

dialogue.speaker: TV Judge
dialogue.line: Will the witness please take the stand?

dialogue.speaker: TV Witness
dialogue.line: I saw everything, your honor.

dialogue.speaker: TV Witness
dialogue.line: The defendant is guilty as charged—

dialogue.speaker: TV Narrator
dialogue.line: [gunshot]

dialogue.speaker: TV Judge
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose we have our verdict.

jokes.text: Hey, I'm trying to eat lunch here.
jokes.setup: Graphic violence on TV during meal
jokes.punchline: Bart's mild annoyance at timing rather than content
jokes.explanation: Good understated reaction showing Bart's casual relationship with TV violence.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Can't a guy enjoy his pork chops in peace?

jokes.text: Jump, Free Willy! Jump. Jump with all your might. Oh, no. Willy didn't make it! And he crushed our boy! Ugh! What a mess. Oh! I don't like this new director's cut.
jokes.punchline: Movie edited to have whale crushing child, Homer's mild criticism
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy subversion of wholesome family film with Homer's understated reaction to child death.

dialogue.speaker: TV Voice
dialogue.line: And the whale, saved from the nets of fishermen, returned to the ocean.

dialogue.speaker: TV Voice
dialogue.line: Free at last, free at last, the majestic creature dove deep into the sea.

dialogue.speaker: TV Voice
dialogue.line: But the whale's sonar was broken, and it couldn't see the little boy playing in the shallow water.

dialogue.speaker: Sound Effect
dialogue.line: CRUNCH

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh! They ruined it!

jokes.text: You're stealing a table? I'm not stealin' it. Hotels expect you to take a few things. It's a souvenir. Ah. Is that my necktie you're wearing? Souvenir.
jokes.punchline: His logic extending to stealing Barney's personal belongings
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Homer's flawed logic leading to personal theft.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's not stealing if it's from a hotel. They expect it!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Homer, that's my wallet.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you're staying at the Hotel of Life, buddy. Finders keepers!

jokes.text: Mom, what if there's a really bad, crummy guy... who's going to jail, but I know he's innocent. Well, Bart, your Uncle Arthur used to have a saying... 'Shoot 'em all, and let God sort 'em out.' Unfortunately, one day he put his theory into practice. It took 75 federal marshals to bring him down. Now, let's never speak of him again.
jokes.punchline: Marge's disturbing family story ending with enforced silence
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy family backstory that escalates perfectly from saying to mass shooting.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mom, I need some moral guidance.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, when I was your age, I did something I wasn't proud of. I told a lie to my parents. They found out, and you know what they did? They made me sit in the corner for three days without speaking. No TV, no radio, nothing. Just silence. And you know what? That silence was so loud, Bart. So incredibly loud. I could hear my own heartbeat. I could hear the house settling. I could hear your grandfather snoring in the next room. And I realized that sometimes the worst punishment isn't what people do to you—it's what you do to yourself by breaking their trust.

jokes.text: Honey, you should listen to your heart and not the voices in your head... like a certain uncle did one gray December morn.
jokes.punchline: Her ominous reference back to violent uncle
jokes.explanation: Good callback maintaining the dark family history theme with ominous phrasing.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: You know, there's a right way and a wrong way to handle these things.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Just like my Uncle Gladstone always used to say... right before the incident.

jokes.text: Even though reopening a trial at this point... is illegal and grossly unconstitutional... I just can't say no to kids.
jokes.punchline: Judge admitting constitutional violation for sentimental reasons
jokes.explanation: Good judicial incompetence humor prioritizing sentiment over law.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: I'm afraid I must admit... there was indeed a constitutional violation in this trial.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: However, I'm going to allow it to stand anyway... for sentimental reasons.

jokes.text: This is an outrage! I am not a clumsy Clouseau-esque waiter! I will-- Whoa! Whoo! Whoo! Ooh! Ow! Ow! Ow! At least there were no big ones.
jokes.punchline: Immediately demonstrates clumsiness with massive accident
jokes.explanation: Perfect physical comedy setup-payoff with waiter immediately proving the point he's denying.

dialogue.speaker: French waiter
dialogue.line: I am not clumsy! I have never dropped anything in my life!

dialogue.speaker: French waiter
dialogue.line: Watch, I will show you my grace and poise.

dialogue.speaker: French waiter
dialogue.line: *crashes into table, shattering dishes everywhere*

jokes.text: Bart, I'm impressed with what you did in there. You testified for the Quimby boy, even though it was putting your own head in a noose. On the other hand, you skipped school. I guess the two things cancel each other out, huh. I'm a small man in some ways, Bart. A small, petty man. Three months detention.
jokes.punchline: His petty punishment despite recognizing heroic act
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment showing Skinner's self-awareness of his pettiness but inability to overcome it.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Simpson, what you did today took real moral courage. You risked everything to do the right thing.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Thanks, Principal Skinner.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: That's why I'm only giving you detention for a month instead of two.

jokes.text: Wait a minute, Bart. Make that... four months detention.
jokes.punchline: His arbitrary increase for no reason
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency showing Skinner's petty vindictiveness but somewhat predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: That'll be detention for a week.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Make that two weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Actually, a month. I'm feeling generous today.

jokes.text: Well, Marge, it was horrible. Everyone was against me in that jury room. But I stood by the courage of my convictions, and I prevailed. And that's why we had Chinese food for lunch.
jokes.setup: Homer describing his heroic jury stand
jokes.punchline: Non-sequitur ending about Chinese food
jokes.explanation: Good Homer non-sequitur showing his tendency to lose track of stories.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I stood up to the judge and said, 'Your Honor, I will not be swayed by the evidence or the facts. I'm voting not guilty!' The whole courtroom gasped. It was my finest moment.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know what I'm in the mood for? Some Chinese food. Yeah, I'll have the number 37.

jokes.text: Give me those! And those.
jokes.punchline: Marge confiscating stolen items including fake glasses
jokes.explanation: Nice callback tying together multiple visual gags from the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, where did you get all these things?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I may have borrowed a few things from the hotel.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Borrowed? Homer, these are stolen! Hand them over right now.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, they're just tiny soaps and—

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: And fake glasses? Homer!

tmdb_overview: Bart can clear Mayor Quimby's nephew of a charge of battery, but only by implicating himself as a hooky player.
insight: Courtroom satire sustains 76 jokes across 22 minutes despite middling impact scores.
